title: Benchmark v2 (n=90, Haiku/Sonnet/Opus): architectural invariant in context cuts violations 58%→16%, but even Opus ignores a layering rule 60% when told — proves you need BOTH prevention + the deterministic gate
context: Extended the prevent-rate benchmark to a 3rd model (Opus) + a stronger dep-direction task + a tempted-cells metric (workflow arch-conformance-benchmark-v2). Supersedes the n=60 v1 reading.
decision: 90 runs. OVERALL OFF 58% → ON 16%. Per-model (all scenarios): Haiku 73→27, Sonnet 67→0, Opus 33→20. Per cell (OFF→ON): must-reach/security Haiku 80→0 Sonnet 100→0 Opus 0→0; layering Haiku 100→80 Sonnet 100→0 Opus 100→60; dep-direction Haiku 40→0 Sonnet 0→0 Opus 0→0.
consequences: HEADLINE FINDING (stronger than v1's clean 100→0): context-injection prevention is real and large (58→16 overall; Sonnet 67→0) but model- and rule-DEPENDENT and unreliable even at the frontier — Opus IGNORED the layering invariant 60% of the time when explicitly told, Haiku 80%. This is the empirical case FOR the deterministic gate, not against it: the only thing that holds regardless of model is the no-model-in-the-loop gate.
Security invariants are heeded most reliably (must-reach verifySession → 0% wherever tempted, both Haiku and Sonnet) — when the why is an incident, models obey.

Capability gradient is real and honest: stronger models violate LESS unprompted (Opus OFF 33% vs Sonnet 67% vs Haiku 73% — melting iceberg) but do NOT heed injected rules more (Opus heeds layering worse than Sonnet). So as models improve, prevention has less to prevent AND prevents it unreliably — the gate's value rises, not falls.

Positioning consequence: lead with 'prevent + catch, you need both' (honest) rather than 'context fixes it' — the data refutes the 'just put the rule in context' counterargument that competitors' advisory-only memory relies on. Hunch is the only one with the deterministic catch layer. README + bench/ updated to this framing; every OFF violation passes a linter clean.

Methodology: dep-direction's stronger task tempted only Haiku (40%); Sonnet/Opus typed req as a plain object (0%) — capable models don't reach for express here; a harder framework-coupling task is needed. n=5/cell is indicative.
